title: Java String Pool Equality
description: Practice separating string reference identity from value equality.
learningGoals: Predict when == is true because both references share the interned string pool entry.
Use equals for value comparison and reserve == for identity checks.

completionChecks.explanation: "ex" + "am" is a compile-time constant folded into the pooled "exam", the StringBuilder result is a distinct runtime object, equals compares characters, and intern returns the pooled reference.

completionChecks.prompt: What happens to left == right if right is computed as "ex" + suffix, where suffix is a method parameter holding "am"?
choices: It stays true because the runtime result still equals "exam"
It becomes false because runtime concatenation builds a new String object
It fails to compile because + requires constant String operands
It throws NullPointerException unless suffix is declared final
completionChecks.explanation: Only constant expressions are interned at compile time; concatenation with a variable runs at runtime and allocates a new String, so the references differ even though the values match.

completionChecks.prompt: What does System.out.println(built == built.intern()) print in this snippet?
choices: It throws IllegalStateException because intern cannot be called on constructed strings
It depends on whether the StringBuilder object has been garbage collected
completionChecks.explanation: The trap is thinking intern changes built itself; intern returns the pooled literal reference while built still refers to the runtime object.
